speaker: interviewee
text: I went back to Prague, as I told you before. But I also went back to Listany. I wanted to go and see what happened to my village, and also to see-- to go to [? Sharobe ?] where my father is buried. So I went to Listany, but my house is not there. I will read you a little story-- it's not really a story-- which I wrote just last week. It came back to me what it felt like driving into Listany. 'When I went back to my village, after half a century, it almost seemed to me like I would be in one of Segal's paintings, flying over the village, and observing all that was not here anymore. The people with whom I played and went to school, the ones who came to one-- to our store, and, of course, my dear mother and brother. It was like a dream. I was there-- I was being driven through the village, and we arrived at the place where our house stood. There was just a patch of grass. I went out of the car. A woman came along, and we asked her who the mayor was. She told us. We-- we went to his house, but he was not there. I wanted to tell him that I grew up in this village, and it was a long time ago. And there is no one who knows me, I'm-- and I do not know anyone who lives here. The pond, which is in front of our house, is dirty looking. We used to swim in it. They planted some trees and shrubs around it. It look-- it looks better, but it is not as I remember it. We drove on up the hill where the school and the church stood. The school got new windows, and the church got a new coat of paint. It brought back memories of my school days of cherries which tasted so good.' From there, we drove to [? Sharobe ?] to find the cemetery where my father is buried. Unfortunately, we drove around and around, and we couldn't find it. There were little woods, there are meadows, there are fields, and we just couldn't find it. They told me it is very overgrown, and it probably looks like a little forest. So what I have to do is I have to write to the Jewish community in [? Pilsen, ?] and ask them when they are planning to do something with it. I stayed only a day in [? Tushgow, ?] where my-- she's a third cousin of mine-- lives. And she didn't feel good. And I went back to Prague, where I stayed the rest of the time. I spent two weeks in Prague, and as I told you before, I hope to go back in the spring. I really had a very nice time going back. It was not as sentimental as I thought. I knew my way around. I could go anywhere. It all looked the same to me. They are doing a great job of cleaning it up. And it's a beautiful city. As for my work in Montreal, I always did volunteer work. I belonged to [NON-ENGLISH], to the Mount Carmel Chapter, where I was many times the president and coordinator. I joined Hadassah in 1951, so that's a long time ago. And I was the youngest member, so you can imagine how young my friends are. Every year, one of them is missing, and it's getting to be very sad. We used to partake in all the activities. We used to go to the bazaar. We worked hard there. But now, nobody has any strength to do it anymore. We cannot do it. We do meet every two weeks in the summer, because in winter, most of them are away, but still around. And so we do meet in summer. It is nice to-- we saw the children growing up, to get married, to have children, grandchildren, great grandchildren. So I still do that. After all, it is work for Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to ask you what work you did? You said you studied fine arts?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What work did you do after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I studied fine art, and I also became a art teacher for preschoolers. I worked as a teacher for preschool for 13 years in Côte Saint-Luc Recreation, at the library in Côte Saint-Luc, and also in Hampstead. It-- I enjoy it greatly. It is very hard work. But I meet now the children I used to teach. They are now in high-- in-- at university. Some of them are married. And they still remember the nice things we did and the wonderful times we had together. After 13 years, it-- I think that there's a burnout in teachers, and they should really not teach always the same thing. Because at the beginning, you're very enthusiastic, and you always think of new things. But when you-- when you do it for a long time, it becomes so ordinary, and then the enthusiasm is gone. I am still very active. I now work-- I used to work for 20 years at Golden Age, where I was in the meat department. We-- we-- in the Golden Age Association, they sell meat to people who are on supplement, and they can buy the meat and chicken at cost. I worked there for 20 years. And now I work at the Jewish Information Referral Service, which is very interesting. People phone for all kind of things. As a matter of fact, yesterday I had a phone call from a woman who was looking for her child. She could not find the number for the missing children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I managed to get it for her. And I also referred her to the [INAUDIBLE] Institute. Perhaps they will be able to help her. We-- I get phone calls from Honolulu about phone numbers. I-- we get phone calls from France, who want to come and visit. They want to know when the Shabbat begins, where they could eat, where they could stay.

speaker: interviewee
text: I get phone calls from bachelors who want to join single clubs, and things like that. I work at the computer. It is not very difficult. It's-- after all, it's only to retrieve the information, so there's no big problem there. And it's rewarding work for me. I go there once a week. I also do, once a week, Tai Chi. I think it's good for my body and for my mind. And so that fills up my week pretty neatly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever visited Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I-- I have visited Israel. Our son, Victor, as I said before, was a competitive swimmer. And he was advisor at the [? Maccabiah ?] as a swimmer. And the last time in 1977, we went. We accompanied him, and it-- it was very lovely. And he wrote to me, even after, a-- a letter, and he said how proud he was that he saw us sitting in the stands when he marched in with the Canadian contingent, and how sad he was that he missed the Olympics by 8/100 of a second, that he couldn't see his parents sitting in the stands when he was swimming.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does he do now?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's a businessman. He lives-- as I said before, he lives in [INAUDIBLE]. That's just outside of San Francisco. His business is a retail business. He's in tuxedo rentals. He owns 28 stores. And he's very busy with his children. His children are his everything. He's busy, busy from morning to night. During the summer, he leaves his office at 3:00 so he can be with them. They all swim, of course. They-- they play soccer, basketball, dance, go to Hebrew school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's a very busy household. His wife is a fifth generation Californian. And they have a very nice home, and they get along very well. I go there twice a week. Twice-- I'm sorry-- twice a year. As a matter of fact, if everything goes well, I will spend two weeks in January there. And from there I will go to Palm Springs for a little while. Do you think of the Holocaust often?

speaker: interviewee
text: There is not one day which goes by when I do not think about it. It's not that I think about it, but I'm reminded. You read the paper. For instance, there's a-- new thing came out that the Allies knew in 1941 what was going on in Russia, that Jews were being killed in Russia in 1941-- not by the Russians, but by the German troops, which went in. And it wasn't the SS who killed them. It was the ordinary German soldier. And so they knew exactly what was going on and didn't do a thing about it. When we were in-- in Auschwitz, we just couldn't fathom that they couldn't send one plane and rip up those tracks so they couldn't get them straight into the ovens.

speaker: interviewer
text: It-- it just needed one plane. They were bombing all over the world. They-- they knew the targets very well. But what? For Jews, they should send a plane? You know, we are now talking about Zaire and Rwanda.

speaker: interviewee
text: If, at that time, when it happened to us, would have been a television, I think all of these things couldn't have happened, because people would have seen it in their living rooms. And they would have seen the atrocities, which were perpetrated against us. Now, they cannot hide it because there's always a camera man there who's filming it. It's coming into your living room, and you just cannot stand looking at those pictures. So when-- I'm very surprised at the United States, who did not want to send soldiers into Zaire to help those poor people. I'm very surprised. I'm very proud to be a Canadian, to see that Canada always sends soldiers wherever they're needed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you belong to a synagogue now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do. I belong to [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe your belief?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am an observant Jew. I do not go as often to synagogue as I really would want to. It's not that I haven't got the time. Somehow it-- I don't know. Perhaps now I-- I will go more. I do go to all the-- whenever there are holidays, I always go to synagogue. I keep a kosher home. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I believe that there is-- I don't know whether we want to call it God or-- there is something that keeps an order in this world. Why we had to suffer as much, that I'm-- I'm asked many times. But, you know, there is no answer. So if I want to believe in something that there is to keep this order, I do have to believe in God. And I cannot question why we had to go through all of that. I don't say-- I don't want to say that there was a reason, or that something good came out of it. I cannot say that. This wouldn't be it. But we do have an Israel today, and I don't think that something like that could have ever happened if we would have had an Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What has become important in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: What has become important? Peace on Earth, good health, friends, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] to get along with one another. I wish I would have the power to make all people equal. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any particular message or statement you'd like to make for people who will see your story and hear it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The message is from me, to everyone who will see it, that we should never forget the Holocaust. We have to fight against those who say that we imagined it, to fight against deniers. I would love to get Mr. Zundel off the air. He's now on the internet, and I don't think that he has any right to be on the internet and poison minds of young people, or anyone. I cannot understand that a man like that can spew his untruth over the whole world. I just cannot understand it. I know there's one man who did that in Germany, and he was arrested. And Mr. Zundel, who lives in Canada, who's not even a Canadian can go on like that. I think that we really have to fight against that. And as long as I have a voice, I will speak about it, mainly to young people. I like to speak to young people. They have to know what happened. The older ones, they should because they were around. But it's important that young people know what happened and that it should never happen again.

speaker: interviewer
text: You speak to children often?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, whenever I'm called by the Holocaust Center, that they have a class, and they want I should speak to them, I try to make-- always make it if they need me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are these children?

speaker: interviewee
text: They have children from-- probably from eight years on. But I like mostly teenagers, because I-- somehow I can relate to them, because I can say, when I was your age, that's what happened to me. And I-- I talk to them about how lucky they are to live in Canada and have all the freedoms, and their worries are whether they go out Saturday night, of course, and if they have Nikes or Gap, or whatever. That when I was their age, my most-- my biggest concern was to stay alive. And the sad part is, in this country are-- not only in this country, but amongst teenagers, that there's so many commit suicide. And that there's no one who can kind of help them out. I mean, for us, when we were in Auschwitz or Birkenau, to commit suicide was a very simple thing. We just have to touch the wire, and we would have been dead. But we wouldn't do a thing like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you'd like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. I think that, for today, that's all I want to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, Mrs. Ivry, thank you very much for sharing your story with us today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for being here with me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is my grandfather on my father's side. His name is Adolf Epstein. The picture was taken probably 1899. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is his wife, Caroline Epstein. She's really his niece. He married his brother's daughter. He said she was so beautiful that he had to have her. And they-- unfortunately, she died in childbirth in 1899. So the picture probably was taken in 1898 in [? Pilsen. ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is my mother and father, Vicktor Epstein and [? Elsa Epstein ?]. And it's probably taken in the early 1920s in Liechtenstein, or Listany. That's before I was born, I think. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my father, Vicktor Epstein, and that's the first World War. And as you can see, he had some medal there. I think it might have been 1917, 1918. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is the village where I was born, and it's called Listany now. At that-- when I was born, it also was called Liechtenstein. As you can see, on the hill there's a church. On the right side, the house-- that long house-- is the house where I was born, my mother was born, my grandmother, my great grandmother. I don't know before that. But when we lived there, it had a new slate roof. Now, unfortunately, it doesn't-- nothing is there. There's a patch of grass there, nothing else. And the village is more or less the same the way you see it now. A few new houses, but otherwise, it's the same little village. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is in front of our house. My father sits on the little carriage there. My mother stand there, and then next to her is my Aunt Mitzi who lived in Vienna. The horse, by that time, is quite an old horse, because it was conscripted into the first-- in the First World War. And after the war we got it. We-- I used to call him Browner, and I used to ride on it without a saddle. He was a very good horse. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is me as a little girl, probably about a year old. And you must wonder where I got those pictures from. I had a cousin in Toronto and uncle in Vienna after the war, and whenever-- whenever they died, that's the only thing I got. They had the pictures. I always wrote to get the pictures. I didn't want they should go in the garbage.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had to pay for it. I had to send a check to Vienna. I had to send a check to Toronto. And this is how I accumulated all of these pictures I had. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the only picture I have with my mother and father and my brother and myself. Myself is Liselotte Ivry, born Epstein. There is my brother to the right. His name is Jans Epstein. Above him, my father, Vicktor Epstein, and to the left, my mother, Elsa Epstein. I think it was taking in 1928, because my father passed away in 1929. And I was, at that time, three and a half. So there it looks like I'm three and my brother's one. So that's probably what it is. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture of myself, Liselotte Ivry, and my brother, Jans Epstein. It was taken in Listany, or Lichtenstein. I believe I was six years old-- five or six. That means my brother was four years old. He doesn't seem to be very happy there. He's pouting. And I do remember the dress very well. It was a brown velvet dress, and the beads were red. This is me, Liselotte Ivry Epstein or Epstein Ivry. This picture was taken in [? Pilsen ?] in 1937. I went there to school. I can remember the color on the dress. It was white, red, and green. I [INAUDIBLE] in Prague when I went to the [INAUDIBLE] school. It did have a little jacket. So I made out of the jacket and the skirt a dress. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is my friend, Hanus Gerst, whom I met in Terezin. His mother had this picture taken just before he left. Unfortunately, he died in January of 1945, on the 15th of January. So close to liberation, and he couldn't make it. His mother still cannot go-- get over it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is ghetto money, which we got in, I believe, 1943. We were about-- we were supposed to buy some things which they had in stores, which really was crazy. Because the things they had, they were selling in the stores, they took from us in the first place, and then they were selling it. They had some toilet articles, I believe. But I really can't recall anymore. But I just wanted to show you how ridiculous it was that they gave us money. And there was nothing what to buy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which camp was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in Theresienstadt, or Terezin. [PAUSES FOR 20 SECONDS] This is one of two volumes of books, which were now printed. It is-- it contains 81,000 names. It is the Jewish people from Bohemia and Moravia to Terezin. They have them listed by transport, and they have all the ones who died and all of the ones who were liberated. And in this book I can find my mother's name-- Epstein-- Epstein Eliska, born 1890, and my brother, Hanus, who was born on the 8th of June, 1927. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a document from 1945. When I came back, I went to my village, to Listany, or Lichtenstein, and asked the mayor to have the people write down what they took out from our house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as you can see, there's different names and different things listed. There is a horse listed. There is a sewing machine listed, a wash stand, a cupboard, all different things. But I really only got back my sewing machine. And for my horse, I got three dozen eggs. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the ID from 1947. It's-- that's-- if I say, that's me. There's my name on it, written on it, Liselotte Epstein, now Ivry. Was a pretty good looking girl then. This is Liselotte Ivry Epstein in Prague in 1949. I used this picture for my passport just before leaving for Canada. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture of my husband, Sidney Ivry, and I'm Liselotte Ivry Epstein. It was taken at our son's bar mitzvah, which was-- I forgot. He was born in 1956-- 1966, '69. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is my daughter and her husband. My daughter's name is Elsa. Her name is now Cox, and his name is John. And on the left is my daughter-in-law's sister, Julie-- I don't remember-- [? Susman. ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was at my son-in-law-- son-in-law's-- at my son's wedding reception in Montreal. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is my son's family. My son, Victor Ivry, his wife Lori Ivry, his three children, Jessica, Sarah, and Claire. It was taken in 1995 near San Luis Obispo on a dude ranch. These are my granddaughters, the children of my daughter, Elsa. On the right is Jeniffer. Her name is now Williams. And on the left is Andrea. Her name is Cox. She wants to be a vet. And the picture on the left was taken in 1995, and the picture on the right was taken in 1993. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is my uncle and aunt. I call them uncle and aunt. They're really a first cousin. He's a first cousin of my late mother. He became chief rabbi of Prague. During the war, he lived in Palestine. And after the war, they wanted to bring someone back who had Czech education. So they brought him back. His name is Dr. Gustav Zicher, and her name was Elsa Zicher. She was originally from Katowice, and I liked him very much and he liked me. I used to go there for Passover Seders, which ended about 3:00 in the morning. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a painting I did, probably 1969 or something like that. I used to go to the dollar concerts at the forum. And while I was sitting there, I looked down at the orchestra. And it came to me that you really cannot see any facial expression. You only could tell the facial color and the way the hair was-- whether it was parted, whether it was down, whether it was light. And so I painted this picture. I had quite good success with it, because there were some people who were interested in it. And I did a few more. Of course, I didn't do them exactly like that. It is done with a wash, a very thin paint. And I enjoy always looking at it, and I still like it after all these years.
